cards.title: How to express gratitude in Arabic
cards.body: To say "thank you" in Arabic, use the versatile word شكراً (shukran, pronounced "shook-ran"). This handy term is recognized across the entire Arabic-speaking world, from Morocco to Iraq. To make it more formal or heartfelt, you can say شكراً جزيلًا (shukran jazilan, pronounced "shook-ran jah-zee-lan"), which translates directly to "thank you very much."

cards.title: Saying "thank you" in Russian
cards.body: To express gratitude in Russian, say Спасибо (spasibo, pronounced "spah-see-bah"). Historically, this everyday word is a contraction of the phrase spasi Bog (Спаси Бог), which translates to "God save [you]." Today, it is used in both casual and formal settings as a universal way to show appreciation.

cards.title: Why an "alibi" literally means you were somewhere else
cards.body: In modern detective stories, an alibi is a story or piece of evidence that proves you could not have committed a crime. The word comes directly from Latin, where alibi is an adverb meaning "elsewhere" or "at another place." If you had an alibi in ancient Rome, you were quite literally somewhere else when the deed occurred.

cards.title: Counting to three in Hindi
cards.body: To count to three in Hindi, say एक (ek, pronounced "ayk"), दो (do, pronounced "doe"), and तीन (teen, pronounced "teen"). Hindi is spoken by hundreds of millions of people, and mastering these three basic numbers is a great first step. They are written in the beautiful Devanagari script, which is read from left to right.

cards.title: Why sharp shapes are "kiki" and round ones are "bouba"
cards.body: In 1929, psychologist Wolfgang Köhler showed people an angular shape and a curvy shape, asking which was "baluba" and which was "takete." Later studies used "bouba" and "kiki." Over 95% of people, regardless of their native language or writing system, map the jagged shape to "kiki" and the rounded shape to "bouba." This suggests human brains map visual shapes to speech sounds in non-arbitrary ways.

cards.title: Wishing someone a "good morning" in Greek
cards.body: To greet someone in Greece during the first half of the day, say Καλημέρα (kalimera, pronounced "kah-lee-meh-rah"). This cheerful word translates literally to "good day" or "good morning," combining kalos (good) and imera (day). It is a polite, warm greeting used with friends and strangers alike until around midday.

cards.title: Why "influenza" is named after the stars
cards.body: Long before modern virology, medieval Italians believed that sudden outbreaks of disease were caused by cosmic forces. The word influenza comes from the Latin influentia, meaning "influence." People believed that a malevolent "influence" flowing from the stars and planets was responsible for causing the sudden epidemics of high fever and coughing.

cards.title: How to politely say "please" in Hebrew
cards.body: When traveling in Israel, the most essential polite word to learn is בבקשה (bevakasha, pronounced "beh-vah-kah-shah"). This versatile Hebrew word means "please" when you are asking for something, but it also does double duty as "you're welcome" when someone thanks you. It is written in the Hebrew script, which is read from right to left.

cards.title: Did wooden shoes really give us "sabotage"?
cards.body: A popular linguistic legend claims that French workers threw their wooden shoes, called sabots, into factory machinery to disrupt production, giving us the word "sabotage." While sabot is indeed the root, lexicographers note that "sabotage" originally referred to working clumsily or making a racket, like clattering around in heavy wooden shoes, rather than throwing shoes into gears.

cards.title: How to say "hello" in Turkish
cards.body: To greet someone in Turkey, say Merhaba (merhaba, pronounced "mair-hah-bah"). Derived from an Arabic phrase meaning "welcome" or "spacious place," this friendly word is the standard, polite way to say hello to anyone you meet. Today, it is written in the Latin-based Turkish alphabet, which was adopted in 1928.

quizzes.question: What is the meaning of the Arabic word 'shukran jazilan'?
quizzes.explanation: Shukran jazilan translates directly to 'thank you very much' in Arabic.

quizzes.question: What did the Latin word 'alibi' literally mean?
quizzes.explanation: In Latin, 'alibi' is an adverb that means 'elsewhere' or 'at another place'.

quizzes.question: Which Greek greeting translates literally to 'good day' or 'good morning'?
quizzes.explanation: Kalimera (Καλημέρα) combines 'kalos' (good) and 'imera' (day) to mean 'good morning'.

quizzes.question: What cosmic connection gave the disease 'influenza' its name?
quizzes.explanation: Influenza comes from the Latin word for 'influence', reflecting the medieval belief that the disease was caused by celestial influence.

quizzes.question: What does the root of 'sabotage' (the French word 'sabot') actually refer to?
quizzes.explanation: 'Sabots' are traditional French wooden shoes. The word 'sabotage' originally referred to working clumsily, like clattering around in them.

guesses.prompt: What percentage of people, regardless of language, match a rounded shape to the word 'bouba' and a jagged one to 'kiki'?
guesses.explanation: About 95% of people consistently make this association, showing that human brains link abstract shapes to speech sounds.
